_stage: CPAL Stage 1 — Wrong-Model Identification + LP Level Classification
_special_case: SUCCESSFUL PROGRESSION — student arrived at L2, advanced to L3 within session

_concept_meta.java_concept: Pass-by-value, iteration. Error: modifying the loop variable in a for-each does not modify the array element.

_input_recap.student_message_round_1: I changed x in the loop but the array didn't change. I thought x was pointing to arr[i] so changing x would change the array. I know I should use an indexed for loop to actually modify it but I want to understand why for-each doesn't work.
diagnostic_phrases_extracted_round_1: I changed x in the loop but the array didn't change
I thought x was pointing to arr[i]
I know I should use an indexed for loop
I want to understand why for-each doesn't work

wrong_model_identification.wrong_model_description: for-each creates a reference to each element. Modifying the variable updates the original through the reference.
wrong_model_identification.wrong_model_origin: Loop variable = reference to original. Works for objects, student assumes it works for primitives too.
wrong_model_identification.matched_signal: I thought x was pointing to arr[i] so changing x would change the array
wrong_model_identification.match_evidence: Verbatim match on the documented FNM-B signal. The 'pointing to' language is the FNM-B signature — this student has a reference model and is applying it to primitives where it doesn't hold.

alternatives_considered.wrong_belief_short: Loop variable IS the array element (no indirection model)
alternatives_considered.rejected_because: FNM-A students don't use 'pointing to' language — they treat x and arr[i] as the same thing. Aisha already has a reference/indirection mental model; her bug is applying it where it doesn't apply.

alternatives_considered.wrong_belief_short: for-each and indexed for are interchangeable for all purposes
alternatives_considered.rejected_because: Aisha already separates them ('I know I should use an indexed for loop'). She's not at FNM-C.

lp_signals_detected_round_1.signal: Knows the rule (use indexed for to modify)
lp_signals_detected_round_1.evidence: 'I know I should use an indexed for loop to actually modify it'
lp_signals_detected_round_1.rubric_match: L2: 'Knows for-each cannot modify primitive array elements. Knows to use indexed for loop for modification.'

lp_signals_detected_round_1.signal: Asks for the mechanism — primed for L3
lp_signals_detected_round_1.evidence: 'I want to understand why for-each doesn't work'
lp_signals_detected_round_1.rubric_match: Boundary L2/L3: explicitly seeks the mechanism the L2 description says they cannot articulate

_intervention_applied.selected_by: RL teaching agent (Q=5.18) — not plateau-forced; not first session at L2
_intervention_applied.summary_of_what_system_did: Drew two boxes side-by-side: the array slots (heap-allocated int[5] with values 1..5) and the loop variable x (a separate stack slot). Showed each iteration: 'JVM reads arr[i], copies the value into x, runs the loop body, discards x at end of iteration. Modification of x never travels back.' Distinguished primitive-value-copy from object-reference-copy explicitly.

_round_2_followup.student_message_round_2: Oh — so x is its own little box, and the JVM copies the value 1 into it, then 2, then 3. Whatever I do to x doesn't matter because the array still has the originals. If I had an array of objects, x would hold a copy of the *reference*, so x would point at the same object — modifying the object's fields would persist, but reassigning x to a different object wouldn't. Is that right?
diagnostic_phrases_extracted_round_2: x is its own little box
the JVM copies the value 1 into it, then 2, then 3
Whatever I do to x doesn't matter
x would hold a copy of the reference
modifying the object's fields would persist, but reassigning x to a different object wouldn't

lp_signals_detected_round_2.signal: Articulates the value-copy mechanism step by step
lp_signals_detected_round_2.evidence: 'JVM copies the value 1 into it, then 2, then 3'
lp_signals_detected_round_2.rubric_match: L3: 'for-each copies each element's value into the loop variable. For primitives, this is a value copy.'

lp_signals_detected_round_2.signal: Extends to object case correctly — distinguishes mutation-through-reference from reassignment
lp_signals_detected_round_2.evidence: 'modifying the object's fields would persist, but reassigning x to a different object wouldn't'
lp_signals_detected_round_2.rubric_match: Reaches into L4 territory: 'Distinguishes object state mutation (visible) from reference reassignment.' Aisha is on the boundary L3/L4 by end of round 2.

plateau_gate._note: Aisha advanced on the first L2 attempt — plateau condition (lp_streak >= 2) never approached.

expert_benchmark_key_ideas: Pass-by-value applies uniformly across method parameters, assignment, and for-each iteration: the receiving slot gets a copy of the source's bits.
For primitives, the bits are the value itself.
For object references, the bits are an address. Copying the address means both slots now point at the same heap object — mutating the object is visible everywhere; reassigning the local slot is not.
There is no pass-by-reference in Java. Every parameter binding is a value copy of either a primitive or an address.

downstream_effects.stage_4_delta_lp: +1 from L2 baseline → reward signal +3.5 to RL agent (delta_lp_bonus + within-session_advancement_bonus)
downstream_effects.stage_5_skg_writes: Update arjun-style cognitive_graph.concept_nodes.foreach_no_modify: mastery 0.55 → 0.84, encoding 'partial' → 'deep', dual_coding 'verbal_only' → 'verbal_and_visual', elaboration false → true. Append two encoding_history entries (round 1 and round 2 timestamps).
downstream_effects.rl_reinforcement: The action 'memory_diagram_with_value_copy' gets credit for the L2→L3 transition at this state class (high curiosity expressed by 'I want to understand why', moderate prior mastery, FNM-B wrong model). Q-value for this (state, action) pair updated upward.
